title: Woven hand towel on a floor loom
subtitle: A pair of cotton plain-weave hand towels woven on a four-shaft floor loom with a hemstitched edge
excerpt: Weave two cotton hand towels on a four-shaft floor loom in plain weave with a broad warp stripe at each end. The project is a classic floor-loom sampler: the warp is straightforward to dress, the weaving is repetitive and good for building even beat and consistent selvedges.
sourceNotes: Cotton towel construction and hemstitching documented in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910, public domain, Internet Archive), and traditional British floor-loom project records.

glossaryTerms.definition: An embroidery stitch worked at the cut end of a woven piece to secure the warp threads and create a decorative heading just above the fringe. The stitching is done on the loom while the warp is still under tension.
The simplest interlacement: each weft thread passes over one warp thread and under the next, alternating each row. Produces a balanced, stable fabric.
The number of warp threads per unit width. Cotton hand towels are typically woven at 20 to 24 ends per 10 cm for a fabric that is dense enough to absorb well but soft enough to dry quickly.

content.text: hand towel is one of the most reliable first floor-loom projects. The
is straightforward, the weaving is rhythmically meditative, and
the ends on the loom gives a professional finish.

items.name: Cotton 8/2 warp yarn in a main colour
Cotton 8/2 weft yarn (same or slightly different colour)
Contrasting cotton for the end stripe

content.text: Measure a warp of 240 ends in 8/2 cotton at 24 epi (about 10 cm per 24 ends), giving a finished woven width of 45 cm after take-up. Allow 3 m total warp length for two towels of 60 cm each plus loom waste and a 5 cm spacer between them. Thread on a straight draw through four shafts and sley 2 ends per dent in a 12-dent reed.

Begin each towel with a 3 cm header in scrap cotton to spread the warp. Then weave 10 rows in a contrasting colour for the end stripe, switch to the main weft colour, and weave 50 cm of plain weave. Finish with another 10 rows of contrasting colour. Weave 5 cm of scrap cotton between the two towels to serve as the spacer.

Before cutting the first towel off the loom, hemstitch along the first woven row with a blunt needle and a length of the weft yarn. Work in groups of 4 warp threads: bring the needle under 4 warps from right to left, then up between the last 2 warp threads of the group and back through the woven rows to lock the group. The hemstitch secures the end and creates the decorative heading for the fringe.

Wash the towels in warm soapy water by hand, pressing gently. The cotton will shrink by about 10 percent in the first wash, which sets the interlacement and softens the fabric considerably. Press flat while damp and leave to dry.

items.problem: The selvedges are pulling in on both sides
items.cause: The weft is being inserted too tightly at each end without enough arc or bubble
items.fix: Insert the weft in a diagonal arc across the width before beating. The arc provides the extra length the weft needs to go around the outermost warp threads without drawing them in.

items.problem: The weft colour stripe at the towel end looks wider on one towel than the other
items.cause: Different beat pressure or weft tension between the two towels
items.fix: Count the picks in each stripe rather than estimating by length. Beat consistently and count to the same number of rows for both stripes.
